Peroral Endoscopic Myotomy as Retreatment for Esophageal Motility Disorders: A Propensity-Matched Analysis from a Western Cohort
  • Source: Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (2026), Vol. X, Issue X
  • Topic: POEM outcomes in patients with prior treatment failure vs treatment-naïve patients
  • Author(s): Alfarone L, de Sire R, Massimi D, Capogreco A, Poletti V, Baldaccini V, Carlani E, Pellegatta G, Jacques J, Hassan C, Maselli R, Repici A
1. Study Overview
  • Design: Retrospective analysis of prospectively maintained cohort
  • Setting: Single high-volume tertiary center (Humanitas Research Hospital, Italy)
  • Period: March 2012 to March 2024
  • Follow-up: Minimum 6 months, median 36 months (range 6–60)
  • Total patients: 564 POEM procedures included
1.1 Study Aims
  • Assess long-term outcomes in patients with prior treatment failure (PTF) vs naïve patients
  • Identify predictors of clinical failure after POEM
1.2 Patient Groups
  • Treatment-naïve group: Primary POEM (no prior treatment)
  • PTF group: Previous treatments including botulinum toxin, Heller myotomy, POEM, pneumatic balloon dilation (PBD)
2. Baseline Patient Characteristics
  • Median age: 57 years (IQR: 44–69)
  • Sex: 57.3% male, 42.7% female
  • Disorder subtypes:
  • Achalasia type I: 12.8% (n=72)
  • Achalasia type II: 76.6% (n=432) -->most common
  • Achalasia type III: 6.4% (n=36)
  • Non-achalasia disorders: 4.3% (n=24)
2.1 Prior Treatment Failure Cohort
  • PTF patients: 108 (19.1% of total)
  • Single prior treatment: 83 cases (76.9%)
  • Multiple prior treatments: 25 cases (23.1%)
2.2 Prior Treatment Types
  • Pneumatic balloon dilation: 42 (39%)
  • Heller myotomy: 40 (37%)
  • Prior POEM: 16 (15%)
  • Botulinum toxin injection: 10 (9%)
2.3 Key Baseline Differences (PTF vs Naïve)
  • PTF patients --> older median age (62 vs 56, p<0.001)
  • PTF --> longer disease duration >5 years (57.8% vs 20.5%, p<0.0001)
  • PTF --> more tortuous/sigmoid esophagus (25.9% vs 13.8%, p=0.009)
  • PTF --> lower pre-POEM Eckardt score ≥7 (47.1% vs 58.6%, p=0.03)
3. POEM Procedure Details
  • Performed under general anesthesia with endotracheal intubation
  • CO2 insufflation used to reduce gas-related complications
  • 4 standardized steps:
  • Submucosal injection in mid-esophagus (anterior or posterior wall)
  • Mucosal incision: 1.5–2 cm longitudinal entry point
  • Submucosal tunnel creation --> at least 2 cm beyond EGJ
  • Selective or full-thickness myotomy from 2 cm distal to incision --> end of tunnel
  • Mucosal entry closure: endoscopic clips
3.1 Special Considerations for PTF
  • For prior endoscopic/surgical myotomy --> myotomy on opposite side
  • Same standardized technique maintained
  • Length of esophageal and gastric myotomy preserved
3.2 Post-Procedure Protocol
  • Single-dose prophylactic antibiotic 30 min pre-procedure
  • Fasting post-POEM --> liquid/smooth diet on postoperative day 1
  • Proton pump inhibitors for 2 months (anti-reflux treatment)
4. Clinical Failure Outcomes
4.1 Primary Outcome Definition
  • Eckardt score (ES) >3 at any follow-up point
  • ES assesses: dysphagia, regurgitation, chest pain, weight loss (0–3 each)
  • Total score >3 = clinically meaningful symptom burden
4.2 Overall Clinical Failure Rates
  • Total failures: 72/564 (12.7%)
  • PTF group: 21.3% vs Naïve group: 10.7% (p<0.01)
  • PTF --> nearly double the risk of clinical failure
4.3 Timing of Clinical Failures
  • At 6 months: 30 (41.7%)
  • At 18 months: 23 (31.9%)
  • At 3–5 years: 10 (13.9%)
  • At ≥5 years: 9 (12.5%) -->late relapses occur
4.4 Independent Predictors of Clinical Failure
  • PTF was the only independent risk factor (HR 1.87, p=0.01)
  • Multiple prior treatments: HR 3.41 (p<0.001) -->significantly higher risk
  • Previous myotomy (surgical or endoscopic): HR 2.66 (p=0.001)
4.5 After Propensity Score Matching
  • 130 matched patients (65 per group)
  • PTF significantly associated with clinical failure: HR 2.20 (95% CI 1.05–5.36, p=0.04)
  • All covariates well-balanced (SMD <0.1)
4.6 Subgroup Analysis by Prior Treatment Type
  • Reference: Previous surgery
  • Pneumatic dilation: HR 0.24 (95% CI 0.08–0.70, p=0.009) --> significantly lower risk
  • Prior POEM: HR 0.18 (p=0.105) --> non-significant lower trend
  • Botulinum toxin: HR 0.84 (p=0.758) --> comparable risk
5. GERD-Related Outcomes
5.1 Assessment Methods
  • Clinical reflux: heartburn or acid regurgitation
  • Objective criteria:
  • Erosive esophagitis on EGD (Los Angeles classification)
  • Grade >B = severe
  • AET >6% on 24-hour pH-impedance monitoring
5.2 Overall GERD Rates (Median 36-Month Follow-Up)
  • Clinical reflux: PTF 28.1% vs Naïve 36.1% (p=0.15)
  • Grade >B esophagitis: PTF 25.4% vs Naïve 33.3% (p=0.14)
  • AET >6%: PTF 36.2% vs Naïve 43.9% (p=0.16)
  • No significant difference between groups
5.3 After PSM - GERD Outcomes
  • Clinical reflux: OR 1.77 (95% CI 0.75–4.15, p=0.193)
  • Grade >B esophagitis: OR 1.52 (95% CI 0.67–3.69, p=0.350)
  • AET >6%: OR 1.72 (95% CI 0.85–3.68, p=0.163)
  • Prior treatment not significantly associated with any reflux endpoint
5.4 Additional Findings
  • No Barrett esophagus reported
  • No peptic stenosis observed
  • No other serious GERD-related complications
  • Prior Heller + fundoplication --> no lower reflux vs non-surgical prior therapy
6. Perioperative Outcomes
6.1 Adverse Events
  • Overall rate: 23/564 (4.1%)
  • PTF group: 2/108 (1.9%) vs Naïve: 21/456 (4.6%)
  • No significant difference between groups (p=0.193)
6.2 Adverse Event Types
  • Pneumoperitoneum: 3 cases
  • Pneumonia: 8 cases
  • Subcutaneous emphysema: 6 cases
  • Esophageal mucosotomy (conservative management): 3 cases
  • Pneumomediastinum: 1 case -->required drainage
  • Pneumothorax: 1 case -->required drainage
  • Pleural empyema: 1 case
6.3 Procedural Time
  • Median: 70 minutes (IQR: 55–85)
  • PTF vs Naïve: 71 min vs 70 min (p=0.812)
  • No significant difference
6.4 Hospitalization Length
  • PTF group: median 2 nights (IQR: 1–3)
  • Naïve group: median 1 night (IQR: 1–3)
  • Significantly longer in PTF (p<0.001)
7. Statistical Methods
  • PSM: 1:1 nearest-neighbor matching, caliper width 0.2
  • Covariates matched: age, gender, ASA score, disease duration, esophageal shape, disorder subtype, pre-POEM Eckardt score
  • Survival analysis: Kaplan-Meier curves + Cox proportional hazards regression
  • Significance threshold: p<0.05
8. Study Limitations
  • Retrospective design --> inherent selection/information biases
  • Single-center study --> limited generalizability
  • Trainee involvement not consistently recorded
  • Long study period (12 years) --> potential technique refinement bias
  • Univariate analysis by era showed no difference in clinical failure (2012–2017 vs 2018–2024)
  • Interval from previous treatment to recurrence --> not systematically available
  • Partial follow-up via telephone interviews --> potential recall/reporting bias
9. Key Conclusions
  • POEM is feasible, safe, and effective in PTF patients
  • Long-term clinical failure rate --> higher in PTF vs naïve (21.3% vs 10.7%)
  • After PSM: PTF --> HR 2.20 for clinical failure (p=0.04)
  • Multiple prior treatments --> HR 3.41 for clinical failure
  • Prior myotomy (surgical/endoscopic) --> HR 2.66 for clinical failure
  • GERD outcomes --> no significant difference between groups
  • Adverse events and procedural time --> comparable between groups
  • Hospitalization --> significantly longer in PTF (2 vs 1 nights)
9.1 Clinical Implications
  • Closer monitoring recommended for PTF patients after POEM
  • Early POEM after initial treatment failure --> may minimize clinical failure risk
  • Most failures occur within 3 years, but 12.5% occur at ≥5 years --> long-term follow-up essential
  • Prior Heller + fundoplication does not reliably prevent reflux after revision POEM
9.2 Future Directions
  • Prospective, multicenter studies needed
  • Extended follow-up to assess long-term durability
  • Investigation of pathophysiological mechanisms in PTF failure
  • Development of novel therapeutic approaches for high-risk groups
